`'`","user":{"login":"mtfishman","id":7855256,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc4NTUyNTY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7855256?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman","html_url":"https://github.com/mtfishman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":7801806106,"node_id":"LA_kwDONPNfxc8AAAAB0QYdGg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/labels/linalg%20triage","name":"linalg triage","color":"B60205","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-03-08T17:58:03Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:42:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hello,

I think it would be nice to be able to have different definitions for `adjoint` and `'` (which I think is not possible currently, except in a local scope).

An example we have is in [ITensors.jl](https://github.com/ITensor/ITensors.jl), where we define `'` as the function `prime` (which means adding a prime level to a tensor index, and has nothing to do with `adjoint` or conjugate transposition of a matrix). So for example:
```julia
julia> using ITensors

julia> i = Index(2)
(dim=2|id=769)

julia> A = ITensor(i', i)
ITensor ord=2 (dim=2|id=769)' (dim=2|id=769)
NDTensors.EmptyStorage{NDTensors.EmptyNumber, NDTensors.Dense{NDTensors.EmptyNumber, Vector{NDTensors.EmptyNumber}}}

julia> A'
ITensor ord=2 (dim=2|id=769)'' (dim=2|id=769)'
NDTensors.EmptyStorage{NDTensors.EmptyNumber, NDTensors.Dense{NDTensors.EmptyNumber, Vector{NDTensors.EmptyNumber}}}

julia> A' == ITensor(i'', i')
true

julia> A' == prime(A)
true
```
I would like to define an explicit conjugate transpose/adjoint operator of an ITensor and use the name `adjoint` (since I can't think of a better name), but my understanding is that even with https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/34634 I can't make different global definitions of `var"'"` and `adjoint` since they are just aliases of each other.

So essentially I would hope to define:
```julia
Base.var"'"(A::ITensor) = prime(A) # Nice notation for `prime`
Base.adjoint(A::ITensor) = swapprime(dag(A), 0 => 1) # Conjugate transpose of matricized ITensor
```

Is this possible in some way right now that I'm not seeing? If not, I could imagine a simple workaround would be to define:
```julia
var"'"(A) = adjoint(A)
```
instead of the current alias:
```julia
const var"'" = adjoint
```
so then they could be defined separately for user types as needed.

Cheers,
Matt","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/917/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/917/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

of complex Hermitian matrix with `NaN`s does not throw error","user":{"login":"mtfishman","id":7855256,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc4NTUyNTY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7855256?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman","html_url":"https://github.com/mtfishman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-06-29T17:16:37Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:42:29Z","closed_at":"2022-10-06T07:32:54Z","author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As the title says, I've found that calling `eigen` on a complex Hermitian matrix with `NaN`s does not throw an error:
```julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra

julia> eigen(Hermitian(fill(complex(NaN), 2, 2)))
Eigen{ComplexF64, Float64, Matrix{ComplexF64}, Vector{Float64}}
values:
2-element Vector{Float64}:
 NaN
 NaN
vectors:
2×2 Matrix{ComplexF64}:
 NaN+0.0im  NaN+0.0im
 NaN+0.0im  NaN+0.0im
```
while I see:
```julia
julia> eigen(Hermitian(fill(NaN, 2, 2)))
ERROR: ArgumentError: matrix contains Infs or NaNs
[...]

julia> eigen(fill(complex(NaN), 2, 2))
ERROR: ArgumentError: matrix contains Infs or NaNs
```

It looks like it could be fixed by adding a call to `chkuplofinite` inside the complex version of `syevr!`.

This is with:
```julia
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.7.3
Commit 742b9abb4d (2022-05-06 12:58 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)
  CPU: Intel(R) Xeon(R) E-2176M  CPU @ 2.70GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-12.0.1 (ORCJIT, skylake)
Environment:
  JULIA_EDITOR = vim
```
but I've checked on Julia 1.7, 1.8, and 1.9 using both OpenBLAS and MKL.

Related to JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl#406.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/931/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/931/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

There may be something I'm missing about why it was written the way it was before, but this new way looks simpler to me, avoids a `Union` type in type inference, and precludes the need for a few type assertions. This showed up "in the wild" in https://github.com/JuliaGPU/Metal.jl/pull/374, where the `Union` type in type inference caused issues for compiling `sqrt(::Complex)` on Apple There may be something I'm missing about why it was written the way it was before, but this new way looks simpler to me, avoids a `Union` type in type inference, and precludes the need for a few type assertions. This showed up "in the wild" in https://github.com/JuliaGPU/Metal.jl/pull/374, where the `Union` type in type inference caused issues for compiling `sqrt(::Complex)` on Apple GPUs.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54869/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54869/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2182988832","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54869#issuecomment-2182988832","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54869","id":2182988832,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6CHcQg","user":{"login":"mtfishman","id":7855256,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc4NTUyNTY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7855256?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman","html_url":"https://github.com/mtfishman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-21T15:39:24Z","updated_at":"2024-06-21T15:39:24Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"That's a good point, I didn't consider cases where `exponent` might not be outputting something of type `Int`. It seems like for that, instead of making it more generic, we should convert to `Int` like the behavior before. In your example where `exponent(::T)` output something like `BigInt`, it would have failed before with a conversion error if the output couldn't get converted to `Int`, so my guess would be that we should keep that behavior. I suppose changing the initialization of `k` back from `k = 0` to `k::Int = 0` would make sure that if `exponent(m)` output something with a type other than `Int` it would get converted to `Int` like before, but maybe there is a better code pattern to do that, like: ```julia k = m==0 ? k : convert(typeof(k), exponent(m)) ```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2182988832/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Here's an example of a custom number type that outputs `BigInt` from `exponent`: ```julia struct MyBigFloat <: AbstractFloat x::BigFloat end Base.exponent(x::MyBigFloat) = big(exponent(x.x)) Base.promote_rule(::Type{MyBigFloat}, ::Type{<:Real}) = MyBigFloat Base.:<(x::MyBigFloat, y::MyBigFloat) = (x.x < y.x) Base.:-(x::MyBigFloat) = MyBigFloat(-x.x) Base.:+(x::MyBigFloat, y::MyBigFloat) = MyBigFloat(x.x + y.x) Base.:-(x::MyBigFloat, y::MyBigFloat) = MyBigFloat(x.x - y.x) Base.:*(x::MyBigFloat, y::MyBigFloat) = MyBigFloat(x.x * y.x) Base.:/(x::MyBigFloat, y::MyBigFloat) = MyBigFloat(x.x / y.x) Base.nextfloat(x::MyBigFloat) = MyBigFloat(nextfloat(x.x)) Base.eps(type::Type{MyBigFloat}) = MyBigFloat(eps(BigFloat)) Base.ldexp(x::MyBigFloat, y::Int64) = MyBigFloat(ldexp(x.x, y)) Base.sqrt(x::MyBigFloat) = MyBigFloat(sqrt(x.x)) ``` and here is the output of `@code_warntype Base.ssqs(MyBigFloat(1.0), MyBigFloat(2.0))` based on the latest commit:

There may be something I'm missing about why it was written the way it was before, but this new way looks simpler to me, avoids a `Union` type in type inference, and precludes the need for a few type assertions. This showed up "in the wild" in https://github.com/JuliaGPU/Metal.jl/pull/374, where the `Union` type in type inference caused issues for compiling `sqrt(::Complex)` on Apple There may be something I'm missing about why it was written the way it was before, but this new way looks simpler to me, avoids a `Union` type in type inference, and precludes the need for a few type assertions. This showed up "in the wild" in https://github.com/JuliaGPU/Metal.jl/pull/374, where the `Union` type in type inference caused issues for compiling `sqrt(::Complex)` on Apple There may be something I'm missing about why it was written the way it was before, but this new way looks simpler to me, avoids a `Union` type in type inference, and precludes the need for a few type assertions. This showed up "in the wild" in https://github.com/JuliaGPU/Metal.jl/pull/374, where the `Union` type in type inference caused issues for compiling `sqrt(::Complex)` on Apple Output

```julia ERROR: ArgumentError: reducing with add_sum over an empty collection of element type Union{} is not allowed. You may be able to prevent this error by supplying an `init` value to the reducer. Stacktrace: [1] _empty_reduce_error(f::Any, T::Type) @ Base ./reduce.jl:320 [2] reduce_empty(::typeof(Base.add_sum), ::Core.TypeofBottom) @ Base ./reduce.jl:351 [3] reduce_empty(op::Base.BottomRF{typeof(Base.add_sum)}, ::Type{Union{}}) @ Base ./reduce.jl:360 [4] reduce_empty_iter @ ./reduce.jl:384 [inlined] [5] reduce_empty_iter @ ./reduce.jl:383 [inlined] [6] foldl_impl(op::Base.BottomRF{typeof(Base.add_sum)}, nt::Base._InitialValue, itr::Tuple{}) @ Base ./reduce.jl:49 [7] mapfoldl_impl(f::typeof(identity), op::typeof(Base.add_sum), nt::Base._InitialValue, itr::Tuple{}) @ Base ./reduce.jl:44 [8] mapfoldl(f::Function, op::Function, itr::Tuple{}; init::Base._InitialValue) @ Base ./reduce.jl:175 [9] mapfoldl @ Base ./reduce.jl:175 [inlined] [10] mapreduce @ Base ./reduce.jl:307 [inlined] [11] sum(f::Function, a::Tuple{}) @ Base ./reduce.jl:535 [12] sum(a::Tuple{}; kw::@Kwargs{}) @ Base ./reduce.jl:564 [13] macro expansion @ ./multidimensional.jl:1625 [inlined] [14] permutedims!(P::Array{Int64, 0}, B::Array{Int64, 0}, perm::Tuple{}) @ Base ./multidimensional.jl:1615 [15] permutedims(B::Array{Int64, 0}, perm::Tuple{}) @ Base ./multidimensional.jl:1600 [16] top-level scope @ REPL[1]:1 ```

+    using Base: Ordering, Forward, ord, lt, tail, copymutable, DEFAULT_STABLE, IteratorSize, HasShape, IsInfinite
+    function Base.sort(v; kws...)
+        size = IteratorSize(v)
+        size == HasShape{0}() && throw(ArgumentError("$v cannot be sorted"))
+        size == IsInfinite() && throw(ArgumentError("infinite iterator $v cannot be sorted"))
+        sort!(copymutable(v); kws...)
+    end

algebra","color":"f2b3db","default":false,"description":"Linear algebra"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-06-29T17:16:37Z","updated_at":"2022-10-06T12:18:45Z","closed_at":"2022-10-06T07:32:54Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As the title says, I've found that calling `eigen` on a complex Hermitian matrix with `NaN`s does not throw an error:
```julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra

julia> eigen(Hermitian(fill(complex(NaN), 2, 2)))
Eigen{ComplexF64, Float64, Matrix{ComplexF64}, Vector{Float64}}
values:
2-element Vector{Float64}:
 NaN
 NaN
vectors:
2×2 Matrix{ComplexF64}:
 NaN+0.0im  NaN+0.0im
 NaN+0.0im  NaN+0.0im
```
while I see:
```julia
julia> eigen(Hermitian(fill(NaN, 2, 2)))
ERROR: ArgumentError: matrix contains Infs or NaNs
[...]

julia> eigen(fill(complex(NaN), 2, 2))
ERROR: ArgumentError: matrix contains Infs or NaNs
```

It looks like it could be fixed by adding a call to `chkuplofinite` inside the complex version of `syevr!`.

This is with:
```julia
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.7.3
Commit 742b9abb4d (2022-05-06 12:58 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)
  CPU: Intel(R) Xeon(R) E-2176M  CPU @ 2.70GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-12.0.1 (ORCJIT, skylake)
Environment:
  JULIA_EDITOR = vim
```
but I've checked on Julia 1.7, 1.8, and 1.9 using both OpenBLAS and MKL.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45860","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45860/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45860/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45860/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45860","id":1289024841,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5M1PVJ","number":45860,"title":"`eigen` of complex Hermitian matrix with `NaN`s does not throw error","user":{"login":"mtfishman","id":7855256,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc4NTUyNTY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7855256?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman","html_url":"https://github.com/mtfishman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-06-29T17:16:37Z","updated_at":"2022-06-29T19:27:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As the title says, I've found that calling `eigen` on a complex Hermitian matrix with `NaN`s does not throw an error:
```julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra

julia> eigen(Hermitian(fill(complex(NaN), 2, 2)))
Eigen{ComplexF64, Float64, Matrix{ComplexF64}, Vector{Float64}}
values:
2-element Vector{Float64}:
 NaN
 NaN
vectors:
2×2 Matrix{ComplexF64}:
 NaN+0.0im  NaN+0.0im
 NaN+0.0im  NaN+0.0im
```
while I see:
```julia
julia> eigen(Hermitian(fill(NaN, 2, 2)))
ERROR: ArgumentError: matrix contains Infs or NaNs
[...]

julia> eigen(fill(complex(NaN), 2, 2))
ERROR: ArgumentError: matrix contains Infs or NaNs
```

It looks like it could be fixed by adding a call to `chkuplofinite` inside the complex version of `syevr!`.

This is with:
```julia
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.7.3
Commit 742b9abb4d (2022-05-06 12:58 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)
  CPU: Intel(R) Xeon(R) E-2176M  CPU @ 2.70GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-12.0.1 (ORCJIT, skylake)
Environment:
  JULIA_EDITOR = vim
```
but I've checked on Julia 1.7, 1.8, and 1.9 using both OpenBLAS and MKL.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44521","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44521/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44521/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44521/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44521","id":1162956260,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5FUU3k","number":44521,"title":"Different definitions for `adjoint` and `'`","user":{"login":"mtfishman","id":7855256,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc4NTUyNTY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7855256?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman","html_url":"https://github.com/mtfishman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-03-08T17:58:03Z","updated_at":"2022-03-08T17:58:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hello,

I think it would be nice to be able to have different definitions for `adjoint` and `'` (which I think is not possible currently, except in a local scope).

An example we have is in [ITensors.jl](https://github.com/ITensor/ITensors.jl), where we define `'` as the function `prime` (which means adding a prime level to a tensor index, and has nothing to do with `adjoint` or conjugate transposition of a matrix). So for example:
```julia
julia> using ITensors

julia> i = Index(2)
(dim=2|id=769)

julia> A = ITensor(i', i)
ITensor ord=2 (dim=2|id=769)' (dim=2|id=769)
NDTensors.EmptyStorage{NDTensors.EmptyNumber, NDTensors.Dense{NDTensors.EmptyNumber, Vector{NDTensors.EmptyNumber}}}

julia> A'
ITensor ord=2 (dim=2|id=769)'' (dim=2|id=769)'
NDTensors.EmptyStorage{NDTensors.EmptyNumber, NDTensors.Dense{NDTensors.EmptyNumber, Vector{NDTensors.EmptyNumber}}}

julia> A' == ITensor(i'', i')
true

julia> A' == prime(A)
true
```
I would like to define an explicit conjugate transpose/adjoint operator of an ITensor and use the name `adjoint` (since I can't think of a better name), but my understanding is that even with https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/34634 I can't make different global definitions of `var"'"` and `adjoint` since they are just aliases of each other.

So essentially I would hope to define:
```julia
Base.var"'"(A::ITensor) = prime(A) # Nice notation for `prime`
Base.adjoint(A::ITensor) = swapprime(dag(A), 0 => 1) # Conjugate transpose of matricized ITensor
```

Is this possible in some way right now that I'm not seeing? If not, I could imagine a simple workaround would be to define:
```julia
var"'"(A) = adjoint(A)
```
instead of the current alias:
```julia
const var"'" = adjoint
```
so then they could be defined separately for user types as needed.

Cheers,

- [`DataStructures.OrderedSet`](https://juliacollections.github.io/DataStructures.jl/latest/ordered_containers), which I believe is where the code from https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/10116 now lives.
- [`Base.BitSet`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/collections/#Base.BitSet)
- [`Base.Set`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/collections/#Base.Set)
- [`OrderedCollections.jl`](https://github.com/JuliaCollections/OrderedCollections.jl)
- [`Dictionaries`](https://github.com/andyferris/Dictionaries.jl), I'm including this here but in retrospect I don't think I had tried it or knew about it.

I didn't do a very systematic job and can't find the code now, but in the end for small sets I concluded it was best to just roll my own version which just directly used `Vector` (which heavily made use of `Base` definitions that use `Set`).

It looks like `Dictionaries.jl` has some relevant [benchmarks of set operations with various data structures](https://github.com/andyferris/Dictionaries.jl/blob/698ab78d0f32272a1f1b21813701a639b77e62c3/benchmark/bench_indices.jl).
